topic: 1.4 Data Types, Data Structures & Boolean Algebra

questions.q: Convert the denary number 156 to 8-bit unsigned binary.
questions.explain: 128 + 16 + 8 + 4 = 156, giving bits set at those place values: 10011100.

questions.q: Convert the 8-bit unsigned binary number 10110101 to denary.
Convert the hexadecimal number 2F to denary.
Convert the hexadecimal number B6 to 8-bit binary.
What is the 8-bit two's complement representation of -20?
questions.explain: +20 is 00010100; invert all bits to 11101011, then add 1 to get 11101100.

questions.q: Which value does the 8-bit two's complement number 11111011 represent?
questions.explain: Invert 11111011 to 00000100 and add 1 = 00000101 (+5), so the value is -5.

questions.q: The unsigned binary number 00001011 undergoes a logical shift left of 2 places. What is the result in denary?
questions.explain: 00001011 is 11; a logical left shift by 2 multiplies by 2^2 = 4, giving 00101100 = 44.

questions.q: An arithmetic right shift of 1 place is applied to the 8-bit two's complement number 11110000. What is the most important effect?
options: A 0 is copied in, changing it to a positive value
The sign bit is copied in, keeping the value negative (divide by 2)
Bits wrap around to the other end
questions.explain: An arithmetic right shift copies the sign bit (1) in from the left, preserving the sign and dividing the signed value by 2: 11110000 (-16) becomes 11111000 (-8).

questions.q: Simplify the Boolean expression A + (A AND B).
questions.explain: By the absorption law, A + (A.B) = A.

questions.q: Which expression is equivalent to NOT(A OR B) by De Morgan's law?
questions.explain: De Morgan's law: NOT(A OR B) = NOT A AND NOT B - break the bar and change OR to AND, negating each variable.

questions.q: In a half adder, how are the Sum and Carry outputs produced?
options: Sum = A AND B, Carry = A XOR B
Sum = A XOR B, Carry = A AND B
Sum = A OR B, Carry = A AND B
Sum = A XOR B, Carry = A OR B
questions.explain: A half adder gives Sum = A XOR B and Carry = A AND B; it has no carry-in.

questions.q: Which data structure is dynamic and stores each element as a node containing data plus a pointer to the next node?
questions.explain: A linked list is dynamic; each node holds data and a pointer to the next node, with the last pointing to null. Arrays and tuples are static.

questions.q: Which traversal of a binary search tree visits the nodes in ascending order?
questions.explain: An in-order traversal (left, root, right) of a BST outputs the values in ascending order.

questions.q: In a hash table, what is a collision?
options: Two keys that hash to the same index
A key that exceeds the table size
A hash function that is not deterministic
questions.explain: A collision occurs when two different keys produce the same index; it is resolved by techniques such as linear probing or chaining.
